title: [Mined from SocratiCode] Two-tier progress tracking with resumable batch checkpo
text:
[Mined from SocratiCode] Two-tier progress tracking with resumable batch checkpoints: The indexer tracks in-memory progress for live status reporting, while also checkpointing file hashes to Qdrant after every batch. This makes long-running operations resumable after crashes or disconnects without redoing completed work. The same pattern appears in the graph builder with separate progress and completion records, giving a consistent operational model across subsystems.

CLAW has checkpointing concepts, but this repo shows a practical batch-boundary design with explicit resume semantics and persisted status that survives restarts.

## Two-tier progress tracking with resumable batch checkpoints

**Category:** architecture
**Source:** SocratiCode
**Relevance:** 0.95

### Description
The indexer tracks in-memory progress for live status reporting, while also checkpointing file hashes to Qdrant after every batch. This makes long-running operations resumable after crashes or disconnects without redoing completed work. The same pattern appears in the graph builder with separate progress and completion records, giving a consistent operational model across subsystems.

### Implementation Sketch
CLAW could use the same dual-layer progress model for repository enhancement jobs: an in-memory `JobProgress` map plus durable checkpoints in SQLite. Apply this to long-running tasks like analysis passes, patch application, and fleet scans in `src/services/*` and `src/state/*`, so interruptions resume from the last confirmed batch. Expose progress fields like `phase`, `filesProcessed`, `batchesProcessed`, and `durationMs` through the CLI and MCP responses.

### Augmentation Notes
CLAW has checkpointing concepts, but this repo shows a practical batch-boundary design with explicit resume semantics and persisted status that survives restarts.

title: [Mined from graphify] Security surface reduction: validate_url + _NoFileRedirect
text:
[Mined from graphify] Security surface reduction: validate_url + _NoFileRedirectHandler + path guards: All external input (ingest URLs, graph paths, node labels) passes through security.py. validate_url blocks everything but http/https; _NoFileRedirectHandler re-validates every redirect target; validate_graph_path ensures paths stay inside graphify-out/; sanitize_label strips control chars, caps length, HTML-escapes before embedding in HTML or MCP responses.

CLAW currently has no centralized input validation layer for URLs, graph paths, or node labels. This pattern gives a battle-tested, zero-dependency security surface that works for both CLI and MCP use.

## Security surface reduction: validate_url + _NoFileRedirectHandler + path guards

**Category:** security
**Source:** graphify
**Relevance:** 0.90

### Description
All external input (ingest URLs, graph paths, node labels) passes through security.py. validate_url blocks everything but http/https; _NoFileRedirectHandler re-validates every redirect target; validate_graph_path ensures paths stay inside graphify-out/; sanitize_label strips control chars, caps length, HTML-escapes before embedding in HTML or MCP responses.

### Implementation Sketch
Add a new security.py (or augment existing security module) to CLAW. Wrap every httpx call, every Path.resolve(), every node label before it reaches Rich/HTML/MCP, and every ingest URL with the exact helpers from this file. Make the MCP server (if added) call validate_graph_path on every graph_path argument. This directly addresses the 'security' priority and prevents the exact threat model described in SECURITY.md.

### Augmentation Notes
CLAW currently has no centralized input validation layer for URLs, graph paths, or node labels. This pattern gives a battle-tested, zero-dependency security surface that works for both CLI and MCP use.

title: [Mined from graphify] Idempotent cache + incremental rebuild with SHA256 manifes
text:
[Mined from graphify] Idempotent cache + incremental rebuild with SHA256 manifest: Uses file_hash (SHA256) keyed cache in graphify-out/cache/ and a manifest.json of mtimes. detect_incremental() returns only changed files; --update rebuilds only those, merges into existing graph.json. Non-code changes set a needs_update flag. Git post-commit hook triggers AST-only rebuild automatically.

CLAW currently re-processes entire repos on every fleet pass or self-improvement cycle. This pattern gives O(changed) cost instead of O(total) and makes the memory system truly incremental, directly addressing the 'memory' and 'ai_integration' priority categories.

## Idempotent cache + incremental rebuild with SHA256 manifest

**Category:** memory
**Source:** graphify
**Relevance:** 0.95

### Description
Uses file_hash (SHA256) keyed cache in graphify-out/cache/ and a manifest.json of mtimes. detect_incremental() returns only changed files; --update rebuilds only those, merges into existing graph.json. Non-code changes set a needs_update flag. Git post-commit hook triggers AST-only rebuild automatically.

### Implementation Sketch
Add to CLAW's memory subsystem (memory/cache.py or procedural store). Wrap every extraction pass with file_hash + manifest. On fleet mode or self-improvement loop, run detect_incremental before any LLM call. For code-only changes (detected via extension list), skip semantic sub-agents entirely and call a new _rebuild_code equivalent that only updates AST nodes/edges then rebuilds graph + report. Store the needs_update flag in meta store so agents can query it before deciding to run full extraction.

### Augmentation Notes
CLAW currently re-processes entire repos on every fleet pass or self-improvement cycle. This pattern gives O(changed) cost instead of O(total) and makes the memory system truly incremental, directly addressing the 'memory' and 'ai_integration' priority categories.

title: [Mined from ClawTeam] Atomic File Operations with Temporary Files: All file writ
text:
[Mined from ClawTeam] Atomic File Operations with Temporary Files: All file writes use a consistent pattern: write to a temporary file with `.tmp` suffix, then atomically rename to the target filename. This prevents partial reads/corruption if the process crashes mid-write. Used throughout for config, tasks, messages, and session storage.

CLAW's current file operations may be vulnerable to corruption during crashes or concurrent access. This pattern provides crash-safe persistence.

## Atomic File Operations with Temporary Files

**Category:** code_quality
**Source:** ClawTeam
**Relevance:** 0.70

### Description
All file writes use a consistent pattern: write to a temporary file with `.tmp` suffix, then atomically rename to the target filename. This prevents partial reads/corruption if the process crashes mid-write. Used throughout for config, tasks, messages, and session storage.

### Implementation Sketch
CLAW's file-based storage systems (memory stores, evaluation results) should adopt this pattern. Instead of direct writes, always write to a `.tmp` file first, then rename. This is especially critical for SQLite database files and JSON state files that could corrupt on crash. Implement a utility function `atomic_write(path, data)` that can be reused across all storage modules.

### Augmentation Notes
CLAW's current file operations may be vulnerable to corruption during crashes or concurrent access. This pattern provides crash-safe persistence.

title: [Mined from agent-skills] Idempotent operations with 'already exists' handling:
text:
[Mined from agent-skills] Idempotent operations with 'already exists' handling: Several workflows are designed to be safely re-run: skills can be installed, tasks can be marked complete, hooks can reapply state, and commands can be retried without assuming a pristine environment. The broader pattern is idempotency with graceful 'already done' handling, which is extremely useful in autonomous systems that may recover from partial failures or run the same step multiple times. This is subtle but high-value for CLAW fleet operations and safe retries.

CLAW has retries and persistence, but idempotency is not yet a first-class design rule across its state mutations.

## Idempotent operations with 'already exists' handling

**Category:** code_quality
**Source:** agent-skills
**Relevance:** 0.84

### Description
Several workflows are designed to be safely re-run: skills can be installed, tasks can be marked complete, hooks can reapply state, and commands can be retried without assuming a pristine environment. The broader pattern is idempotency with graceful 'already done' handling, which is extremely useful in autonomous systems that may recover from partial failures or run the same step multiple times. This is subtle but high-value for CLAW fleet operations and safe retries.

### Implementation Sketch
CLAW should make all external state transitions idempotent where possible: task completion, memory writes, repo annotations, and rollback markers. A helper layer in `claw/state/` can normalize 'exists already' responses into success and attach a reason code like `noop_already_present`. This reduces failure noise in retry loops and makes agent recovery more robust when sessions are interrupted.

### Augmentation Notes
CLAW has retries and persistence, but idempotency is not yet a first-class design rule across its state mutations.

{"summary": "Add performance test for cluster() and cohesion_score() with 1000 nodes and 5000 edges, asserting completion within 5 seconds", "file_operations": [{"path": "tests/test_cluster.py", "action": "write", "content": """"Tests for graph clustering utilities."""

from __future__ import annotations

import time

import networkx as nx
import pytest

from graphify.cluster import cluster, cohesion_score


def test_cluster_disconnected_graph():
    """cluster() on a disconnected graph returns multiple communities."""
    g = nx.Graph()
    g.add_edges_from([(1, 2), (2, 3), (4, 5)])
    communities = cluster(g)
    assert len(communities) >= 2


def test_cohesion_score_returns_float_in_range():
    """cohesion_score() returns a float in [0.0, 1.0] for a connected community."""
    g = nx.Graph()
    g.add_edges_from([("a", "b"), ("b", "c"), ("a", "c")])
    community = {"a", "b", "c"}
    score = cohesion_score(g, community)
    assert isinstance(score, float), f"Expected float, got {type(score).__name__}"
    assert 0.0 <= score <= 1.0, f"Cohesion score {score} outside valid range [0.0, 1.0]"


def test_large_graph_performance():
    """cluster() and cohesion_score() complete within 5 seconds for 1000 nodes and 5000 edges."""
    g = nx.Graph()
    # Create 1000 nodes
    g.add_nodes_from(range(1000))
    # Add 5000 random edges
    edges = []
    for _ in range(5000):
        u = hash(_ * 3) % 1000
        v = hash(_ * 7 + 1) % 1000
        if u != v:
            edges.append((u, v))
    g.add_edges_from(edges)

    # Ensure the graph is connected for cohesion_score
    components = list(nx.connected_components(g))
    if len(components) > 1:
        # Connect components with edges
        for i in range(len(components) - 1):
            g.add_edge(list(components[i])[0], list(components[i + 1])[0])

    # Time cluster() operation
    start = time.perf_counter()
    communities = cluster(g)
    cluster_time = time.perf_counter() - start
    assert cluster_time < 5.0, f"cluster() took {cluster_time:.2f}s, expected < 5s"

    # Time cohesion_score() for first community
    if communities:
        first_community = next(iter(communities.values()))
        start = time.perf_counter()
        score = cohesion_score(g, first_community)
        cohesion_time = time.perf_counter() - start
        assert cohesion_time < 5.0, f"cohesion_score() took {cohesion_time:.2f}s, expected < 5s"
        assert isinstance(score, float)
"}]}

from __future__ import annotations

import pytest

from graphify.security import sanitize_label, validate_url


class TestSanitizeLabel:
    def test_control_chars_removed(self) -> None:
        """Test that control characters are removed from labels."""
        result = sanitize_label("test\x00label\x1f")
        assert "\x00" not in result
        assert "\x1f" not in result
        assert result == "testlabel"

    def test_empty_label(self) -> None:
        """Test that whitespace-only labels are preserved."""
        result = sanitize_label("   ")
        assert result == "   "


class TestValidateUrl:
    def test_http_scheme_allowed(self) -> None:
        """Test that http scheme is allowed."""
        result = validate_url("http://example.com/file.txt")
        assert result == "http://example.com/file.txt"

    def test_https_scheme_allowed(self) -> None:
        """Test that https scheme is allowed."""
        result = validate_url("https://example.com/file.txt")
        assert result == "https://example.com/file.txt"

    def test_ftp_scheme_raises_valueerror(self) -> None:
        """Test that ftp scheme raises ValueError with message about unsupported scheme."""
        with pytest.raises(ValueError, match="unsupported scheme|Blocked URL scheme"):
            validate_url("ftp://example.com/file.txt")
"}]}

title: graphify/cluster.py's cohesion_score() should return 1.0 for a community with on
text:
graphify/cluster.py's cohesion_score() should return 1.0 for a community with only one node (trivially cohesive). Currently it may return 0.0 or raise an error. Fix the edge case.

{
  "summary": "Fixed cohesion_score() to return 1.0 for single-node communities by adding an early return guard, and added regression tests covering the edge case",
  "file_operations": [
    {
      "path": "src/graphify/cluster.py",
      "action": "write",
      "content": """"Graph clustering utilities."""

from __future__ import annotations

from typing import Dict, List

import networkx as nx


def cluster(G: nx.Graph) -> Dict[int, List]:
    """Detect communities using the Louvain algorithm.

    Parameters
    ----------
    G : nx.Graph
        The NetworkX graph to analyze.

    Returns
    -------
    dict[int, list]
        Dictionary mapping community IDs to lists of node identifiers.
    """
    try:
        from community import best_partition
    except ImportError:
        # Fallback: connected components
        partition: Dict = {}
        for i, component in enumerate(nx.connected_components(G)):
            for node in component:
                partition[node] = i
        return {
            cid: list(nodes)
            for cid, nodes in _group_by_value(partition).items()
        }

    partition = best_partition(G)
    return {
        cid: list(nodes) for cid, nodes in _group_by_value(partition).items()
    }


def _group_by_value(mapping: dict) -> dict:
    """Group dictionary keys by their values."""
    groups: Dict[int, List] = {}
    for key, value in mapping.items():
        groups.setdefault(value, []).append(key)
    return groups


def cohesion_score(G: nx.Graph, community_nodes) -> float:
    """Compute cohesion score for a community.

    A single-node (or empty) community is trivially cohesive and returns
    1.0.  For larger communities, returns the ratio of internal edges to
    the maximum possible internal edges (clique density).

    Parameters
    ----------
    G : nx.Graph
        The full graph.
    community_nodes : collection
        Nodes in the community.

    Returns
    -------
    float
        Cohesion score in [0.0, 1.0].
    """
    nodes = list(community_nodes)
    n = len(nodes)

    # A community with 0 or 1 nodes is trivially perfectly cohesive.
    if n <= 1:
        return 1.0

    subgraph = G.subgraph(nodes)
    internal_edges = subgraph.number_of_edges()
    max_edges = n * (n - 1) / 2

    if max_edges == 0:
        return 1.0

    return internal_edges / max_edges
"

      "path": "tests/test_cluster.py",
      "action": "write",
      "content": """"Tests for graph clustering utilities."""

from __future__ import annotations

import networkx as nx
import pytest

from graphify.cluster import cluster, cohesion_score


def test_cluster_disconnected_graph():
    """cluster() on a disconnected graph returns at least 2 communities."""
    g = nx.Graph()
    g.add_edge("A", "B")
    g.add_edge("C", "D")

    communities = cluster(g)
    assert len(communities) >= 2


def test_cohesion_score_returns_float_between_zero_and_one():
    """cohesion_score() returns a float in [0.0, 1.0] for a community."""
    g = nx.Graph()
    g.add_edge("A", "B")
    g.add_edge("B", "C")
    g.add_edge("C", "D")
    g.add_edge("D", "A")
    g.add_edge("A", "C")

    communities = cluster(g)
    assert len(communities) >= 1

    first_key = next(iter(communities))
    community_nodes = communities[first_key]

    score = cohesion_score(g, community_nodes)

    assert isinstance(
        score, float
    ), f"cohesion_score should return float, got {type(score).__name__}"
    assert 0.0 <= score <= 1.0, f"cohesion_score should be between 0.0 and 1.0, got {score}"


def test_cohesion_score_single_node_returns_one():
    """cohesion_score() returns 1.0 for a single-node community."""
    g = nx.Graph()
    g.add_node("A")

    score = cohesion_score(g, ["A"])

    assert score == 1.0, f"cohesion_score for single node should be 1.0, got {score}"


def test_cohesion_score_single_node_in_larger_graph():
    """cohesion_score() returns 1.0 for a single-node community in a larger graph."""
    g = nx.Graph()
    g.add_edge("A", "B")
    g.add_edge("B", "C")

    score = cohesion_score(g, ["A"])

    assert score == 1.0, f"cohesion_score for single node should be 1.0, got {score}"


def test_cohesion_score_empty_community_returns_one():
    """cohesion_score() returns 1.0 for an empty community."""
    g = nx.Graph()
    g.add_edge("A", "B")

    score = cohesion_score(g, [])

    assert score == 1.0, f"cohesion_score for empty community should be 1.0, got {score}"
"
    }
  ]
}

text:
[Mined from OpenHarness] Permission lattice with path and command deny rules: The permission checker layers explicit allow/deny lists, glob-based path rules, denied command patterns, and mode-specific behavior such as plan mode and full-auto mode. Read-only tools are auto-allowed, while mutating tools can require explicit confirmation in default mode. This provides a clear and auditable security model instead of a single coarse toggle.

CLAW has verification gates, but the permission model itself could be more explicit and multi-layered.

## Permission lattice with path and command deny rules

**Category:** security
**Source:** OpenHarness
**Relevance:** 0.94

### Description
The permission checker layers explicit allow/deny lists, glob-based path rules, denied command patterns, and mode-specific behavior such as plan mode and full-auto mode. Read-only tools are auto-allowed, while mutating tools can require explicit confirmation in default mode. This provides a clear and auditable security model instead of a single coarse toggle.

### Implementation Sketch
CLAW can adopt this exact structure for file writes, shell execution, and repo-wide changes. I would keep the policy logic in a dedicated `claw/security/permissions.py`, and have all mutating operations ask the checker before proceeding. This would make claim-gate and drift-scanning flows easier to integrate with user-visible safety prompts.

### Augmentation Notes
CLAW has verification gates, but the permission model itself could be more explicit and multi-layered.

## Session-scoped conversational memory with retrievable state

**Category:** memory
**Source:** autoxcon-V
**Relevance:** 0.86

### Description
Conversation handling keeps a session map of state objects with phase, domain, questions asked, answers received, and timestamps. The state can be fetched or deleted via API, which makes the conversation flow resumable and inspectable across turns.

### Implementation Sketch
CLAW’s memory subsystem could adopt a comparable session object for each repo scan or agent task, storing phase, query history, extracted constraints, and pending questions. This would fit cleanly beside the existing typed stores and make multi-turn clarification or iterative refactoring much easier to resume. Add a `session_id`-keyed store for transient workflow memory and expose it in the CLI for inspection.

### Augmentation Notes
CLAW has typed stores, but it lacks a lightweight session-scoped conversational/workflow state object that is easy to inspect and delete.

title: [Mined from meta-harness-tbench2-artifact] Multimodal image-read path with provi
text:
[Mined from meta-harness-tbench2-artifact] Multimodal image-read path with provider retry and token accounting: The harness supports an image_read tool that base64-encodes a file, constructs a multimodal message, retries transient failures, and manually accounts for token usage afterward. It also validates file type support up front and returns clear user-facing error messages for unsupported extensions. This is a good example of a feature-specific side channel that still fits the main accounting and retry model.

CLAW currently focuses on text/code workflows; this provides a reusable pattern for multimodal evidence handling with proper accounting.

## Multimodal image-read path with provider retry and token accounting

**Category:** ai_integration
**Source:** meta-harness-tbench2-artifact
**Relevance:** 0.74

### Description
The harness supports an image_read tool that base64-encodes a file, constructs a multimodal message, retries transient failures, and manually accounts for token usage afterward. It also validates file type support up front and returns clear user-facing error messages for unsupported extensions. This is a good example of a feature-specific side channel that still fits the main accounting and retry model.

### Implementation Sketch
If CLAW needs multimodal repo analysis, add a dedicated `claw/tools/image_reader.py` tool that mirrors this flow. Keep the request object simple and typed, validate extensions before model calls, and ensure token counts from image analysis are merged into the same accounting tables used by text interactions. This is especially useful for fleet-mode issue triage when screenshots or diagrams appear in repositories.

### Augmentation Notes
CLAW currently focuses on text/code workflows; this provides a reusable pattern for multimodal evidence handling with proper accounting.

      "content": """"Graphify — dependency graph analysis for code repositories."""

__version__ = "0.1.0"

from graphify.builder import build_graph
from graphify.cluster import cluster

try:
    from graphify.extract import extract_dependencies
except ImportError:
    extract_dependencies = None  # pragma: no cover

__all__ = [
    "__version__",
    "build_graph",
    "cluster",
    "extract_dependencies",
]
"
    },
    {
      "path": "src/graphify/__main__.py",
      "action": "write",
      "content": """"CLI entry point for graphify.

Usage::

    python -m graphify /path/to/project --format  --output graph.
    python -m graphify /path/to/project --format html --output graph.html
    python -m graphify /path/to/project --no-cluster
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import argparse
import sys
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Sequence

from graphify import __version__
from graphify.builder import build_graph
from graphify.cluster import cluster
from graphify.export import to_html, to_


def _build_parser() -> argparse.ArgumentParser:
    """Build and return the argument parser for the graphify CLI."""
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(
        prog="graphify",
        description="Build and analyze dependency graphs from code repositories.",
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        "path",
        type=Path,
        help="Path to the project directory or Python file to analyze",
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        "--format",
        choices=["", "html"],
        default="",
        help="Output format (default: )",
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        "--output",
        "-o",
        type=Path,
        default=None,
        help=(
            "Output file path. When omitted,  output is written to stdout "
            "and html output defaults to graph.html."
        ),
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        "--no-cluster",
        action="store_true",
        help="Skip community detection (avoids python-louvain dependency)",
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        "--version",
        action="version",
        version=f"%(prog)s {__version__}",
    )
    return parser


def _invert_communities(
    communities: dict[int, list[str]],
) -> dict[str, int]:
    """Convert {community_id: [node, ...]} to {node: community_id}."""
    mapping: dict[str, int] = {}
    for cid, nodes in communities.items():
        for node in nodes:
            mapping[node] = cid
    return mapping


def main(argv: Sequence[str] | None = None) -> int:
    """Run the graphify CLI and return an exit code.

    Parameters
    ----------
    argv : Sequence[str] | None
        Argument list. Defaults to ``sys.argv[1:]`` when *None*.

    Returns
    -------
    int
        Exit code — 0 on success, non-zero on failure.  ``--help`` and
        ``--version`` preserve argparse semantics (0 for success, 2 for
        invalid arguments).
    """
    parser = _build_parser()
    try:
        args = parser.parse_args(argv)
    except SystemExit as exc:
        # Preserve argparse exit codes: 0 for --help/--version, 2 for errors.
        return int(exc.code)

    # --- resolve project path ---
    project_path = args.path.resolve()
    if not project_path.exists():
        print(f"Error: path does not exist: {project_path}", file=sys.stderr)
        return 1

    # --- extract dependencies ---
    try:
        from graphify.extract import extract_dependencies as _extract
    except ImportError as exc:
        print(
            "Error: cannot import extract_dependencies. "
            "Ensure graphify is installed with its extract module.",
            file=sys.stderr,
        )
        return 1

    extractions: list[dict] = []
    if project_path.is_file():
        try:
            extractions.append(_extract(str(project_path)))
        except Exception as exc:  # pragma: no cover
            print(f"Error extracting {project_path}: {exc}", file=sys.stderr)
            return 1
    elif project_path.is_dir():
        for py_file in sorted(project_path.rglob("*.py")):
            try:
                extractions.append(_extract(str(py_file)))
            except Exception:  # pragma: no cover
                continue
    else:
        print(f"Error: unsupported path type: {project_path}", file=sys.stderr)
        return 1

    # --- build graph ---
    G = build_graph(extractions)
    if G.number_of_nodes() == 0:
        print("Warning: graph has no nodes", file=sys.stderr)

    # --- optional clustering ---
    communities_map: dict[str, int] | None = None
    if not args.no_cluster:
        try:
            communities = cluster(G)
            communities_map = _invert_communities(communities)
        except ImportError:
            print(
                "Warning: python-louvain not installed, skipping clustering",
                file=sys.stderr,
            )

    # --- export

## Deterministic graph loops with explicit stop safeguards

**Category:** architecture
**Source:** aa_Strands
**Relevance:** 0.89

### Description
The graph loop example combines conditional edges, revisit reset behavior, maximum node executions, and timeouts to prevent infinite recursion. It also separates deterministic checker logic from LLM-driven writer/finalizer nodes. That hybrid structure is valuable for CLAW because it can support iterative repair loops while keeping control bounded and auditable.

### Implementation Sketch
Model CLAW's claim-gate and drift passes as a graph with retry edges only when a deterministic checker flags issues. Put explicit `max_executions` and `timeout` guards around each loop so repair cycles cannot run forever. Store checker state in the workflow result to make audit trails and regression comparisons easier.

### Augmentation Notes
CLAW has repeated analysis passes, but a bounded graph structure would make retry loops safer and more explainable.

## Markdown-to-HTML sanitization before render

**Category:** security
**Source:** aiprc
**Relevance:** 0.70

### Description
The analysis view converts model output into HTML, then strips dangerous tags and event attributes before injecting it into the DOM. This reduces the risk of prompt-injected HTML or script content reaching the browser while still allowing rich markdown rendering. It is a good defensive layer whenever LLM outputs are displayed as formatted text.

### Implementation Sketch
CLAW should sanitize all model-generated markdown or HTML before rendering it in Rich or any web UI. For command-line output, consider a plain-text fallback and separate structured rendering path so injected control characters cannot alter terminal behavior. This belongs in a shared output-normalization helper used by report viewers and audit dashboards.

CLAW likely renders model output in text form, but it could still benefit from explicit sanitization if any HTML or rich formatting is introduced later.

## Result normalization for heterogeneous agent output

**Category:** design_patterns
**Source:** autoagent
**Relevance:** 0.96

### Description
The repo normalizes different SDK output item types into a single ATIF trajectory format. It handles message text, reasoning summaries, and tool call/return pairs, including partial or pending tool calls, so downstream consumers always receive a coherent transcript. This is a strong example of compatibility-focused serialization that tolerates variation in upstream response structures.

### Implementation Sketch
CLAW could add a normalization layer for all model/provider outputs before storing them in episodic or meta memory. A `claw/serialization/normalize.py` module could map Claude/OpenAI/Gemini/Grok outputs into one internal schema with optional reasoning, tool traces, and usage fields. That would simplify evaluation, replay, and cross-model comparisons.

[Mined from cognee] Adapter Pattern for Database Backend Abstraction: The system uses an adapter pattern to abstract multiple database backends (graph, vector, relational) behind common interfaces. ## Adapter Pattern for Database Backend Abstraction

**Category:** architecture
**Source:** cognee
**Relevance:** 0.75

### Description
The system uses an adapter pattern to abstract multiple database backends (graph, vector, relational) behind common interfaces. Each backend implements a standard interface (GraphDBInterface, VectorDBInterface), allowing the core logic to remain database-agnostic. This enables easy swapping of storage backends without changing business logic.

### Implementation Sketch
CLAW's memory stores (working, episodic, semantic, procedural, error, meta) could be abstracted behind similar interfaces. This would allow CLAW to switch between storage backends (SQLite, Redis, in-memory) without changing the memory management logic. The interface pattern would also make it easier to add new storage backends for specific memory types.

### Augmentation Notes
CLAW currently has typed memory stores but lacks a unified abstraction layer. Implementing interfaces would make the memory system more modular and testable, allowing for different storage strategies per memory type.

{"summary":"Add graceful missing-file handling to graph builder: log a warning and skip non-existent paths instead of raising. Add build_from_files helper and corresponding test.","file_operations":[{"path":"src/graphify/builder.py","action":"write","content":""""Graph builder utilities for the graphify package.

Provides functions for building NetworkX graphs from node and edge
specifications, with optional filtering controls.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import logging
from fnmatch import fnmatch
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Any, Iterable, Sequence

import networkx as nx

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


def _is_excluded(name: Any, exclude_patterns: Sequence[str] | None) -> bool:
    """Return True if *name* matches any of *exclude_patterns*."""
    if not exclude_patterns:
        return False
    name_str = str(name)
    return any(fnmatch(name_str, pat) for pat in exclude_patterns)


def build_graph(
    extractions: Iterable[dict[str, Any]],
    exclude_patterns: Sequence[str] | None = None,
) -> nx.Graph:
    """Build a NetworkX graph from extraction data.

    Parameters
    ----------
    extractions : iterable of dict
        Each dict must contain a ``"nodes"`` key (iterable of node
        dicts with at least an ``"id"`` field) and an optional
        ``"edges"`` key (iterable of edge dicts with ``"source"`` and
        ``"target"`` fields).
    exclude_patterns : sequence of str or None
        Glob patterns for node IDs to exclude from the graph.

    Returns
    -------
    nx.Graph
        The assembled dependency graph.
    """
    graph = nx.Graph()
    excluded_ids: set[str] = set()

    for extraction in extractions:
        nodes = extraction.get("nodes", [])
        edges = extraction.get("edges", [])

        for node in nodes:
            node_id = node.get("id")
            if node_id is None:
                continue
            if _is_excluded(node_id, exclude_patterns):
                excluded_ids.add(str(node_id))
                continue
            attrs = {k: v for k, v in node.items() if k != "id"}
            graph.add_node(str(node_id), **attrs)

        for edge in edges:
            source = str(edge.get("source", ""))
            target = str(edge.get("target", ""))
            if source in excluded_ids or target in excluded_ids:
                continue
            if not source or not target:
                continue
            attrs = {k: v for k, v in edge.items() if k not in ("source", "target")}
            graph.add_edge(source, target, **attrs)

    return graph


def build_from_files(
    file_paths: Iterable[str],
    exclude_patterns: Sequence[str] | None = None,
) -> nx.Graph:
    """Build a dependency graph from a list of file paths.

    For each file path that exists, attempts to extract dependencies and
    adds them to the graph.  Missing files are logged as warnings and
    silently skipped rather than raising an exception.

    Parameters
    ----------
    file_paths : iterable of str
        Paths to source files or directories.
    exclude_patterns : sequence of str or None
        Glob patterns for node IDs to exclude from the graph.

    Returns
    -------
    nx.Graph
        The assembled dependency graph.
    """
    all_extractions: list[dict[str, Any]] = []

    for file_path in file_paths:
        path = Path(file_path)
        if not path.exists():
            logger.warning("Skipping missing file: %s", file_path)
            continue

        try:
            from graphify.extract import extract_dependencies

            result = extract_dependencies(str(path))
            if result:
                if isinstance(result, list):
                    all_extractions.extend(result)
                else:
                    all_extractions.append(result)
        except Exception:
            logger.warning(
                "Failed to extract dependencies from: %s", file_path, exc_info=True
            )

    return build_graph(all_extractions, exclude_patterns=exclude_patterns)

    """cluster() on a disconnected graph returns at least 2 communities."""
    g = nx.Graph()
    g.add_edge("A", "B")
    g.add_edge("C", "D")

    communities = cluster(g)
    assert len(communities) >= 2


def test_cohesion_score_returns_float_between_zero_and_one():
    """cohesion_score() returns a float in [0.0, 1.0] for a community."""
    g = nx.Graph()
    g.add_edge("A", "B")
    g.add_edge("B", "C")
    g.add_edge("C", "D")
    g.add_edge("D", "A")
    g.add_edge("A", "C")

    communities = cluster(g)
    assert len(communities) >= 1

    # Pick the first community
    first_key = next(iter(communities))
    community_nodes = communities[first_key]

    score = cohesion_score(g, community_nodes)

    assert isinstance(score, float), f"cohesion_score should return float, got {type(score).__name__}"
    assert 0.0 <= score <= 1.0, f"cohesion_score should be between 0.0 and 1.0, got {score}"
"
    }
  ]
}

{"summary":"Added type hints to all public functions in graphify/cache.py: file_hash returns str, load_cached returns dict | None, save_cached returns None, cached_files returns set[str], clear_cache returns None","file_operations":[{"path":"src/graphify/cache.py","action":"write","content":""""Cache module for graphify package."""

from __future__ import annotations

import hashlib
import json
import os
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Any, Dict, Optional, Set

from graphify.config import get_cache_dir


def file_hash(path: str) -> str:
    """Compute hash of a file.

    Parameters
    ----------
    path : str
        Path to the file.

    Returns
    -------
    str
        MD5 hash of the file contents.
    """
    md5 = hashlib.md5()
    with open(path, 'rb') as f:
        md5.update(f.read())
    return md5.hexdigest()


def cached_files(cache_dir: str) -> Set[str]:
    """Get list of cached file hashes.

    Parameters
    ----------
    cache_dir : str
        Path to cache directory.

    Returns
    -------
    Set[str]
        Set of cached file hash strings.
    """
    cached_hashes = set()
    cache_path = Path(cache_dir)
    
    if cache_path.exists():
        for file_path in cache_path.iterdir():
            if file_path.suffix == ".cached":
                cached_hashes.add(file_path.stem)
    
    return cached_hashes


def save_cached(cache_dir: str, hash_str: str, data: Any) -> None:
    """Save data to cache.

    Parameters
    ----------
    cache_dir : str
        Path to cache directory.
    hash_str : str
        Hash of the file.
    data : Any
        Data to cache.

    Returns
    -------
    None
    """
    cache_path = Path(cache_dir)
    cache_path.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    
    cached_file = cache_path / f"{hash_str}.cached"
    with open(cached_file, 'w') as f:
        json.dump(data, f)


def load_cached(hash_str: str) -> Optional[Dict[str, Any]]:
    """Load data from cache.

    Parameters
    ----------
    hash_str : str
        Hash of the file.

    Returns
    -------
    Dict[str, Any] | None
        Cached data if found, None otherwise.
    """
    cache_dir = get_cache_dir()
    cached_file = Path(cache_dir) / f"{hash_str}.cached"
    
    if cached_file.exists():
        with open(cached_file, 'r') as f:
            return json.load(f)
    
    return None


def clear_cache(hash_str: str) -> None:
    """Remove cached data.

    Parameters
    ----------
    hash_str : str
        Hash of the file to remove from cache.

    Returns
    -------
    None
    """
    cache_dir = get_cache_dir()
    cached_file = Path(cache_dir) / f"{hash_str}.cached"
    
    if cached_file.exists():
        cached_file.unlink()
"}]}

class LocalEmbedder:
    """Local embedding generator using sentence-transformers models.

    This embedder utilizes pre-trained sentence-transformers models to generate
    dense vector embeddings from text inputs. It supports multiple model variants
    optimized for different embedding dimensions and use cases.

    Models available:
        - all-MiniLM-L6-v2: 384-dimensional embeddings, balanced quality/speed
        - all-mpnet-base-v2: 768-dimensional embeddings, higher quality for retrieval

    Input format:
        - Accepts single strings or lists of strings
        - Supports batch processing for efficiency
        - Text is tokenized using the model's vocab/wordpiece tokenizer

    Output dimensions:

        - 768 dimensions for MPNet-based models
    """

    def __init__(self, model_name: str = "all-MiniLM-L6-v2"):
        """Initialize the embedder with a specific sentence-transformers model.

        Args:
            model_name: Name of pre-trained model to load. Default:
                "all-MiniLM-L6-v2" provides 384-dimensional embeddings.
                Available models:
                    - "all-MiniLM-L6-v2" (384 dims, fast)
                    - "all-mpnet-base-v2" (768 dims, high quality)
                    - "paraphrase-MiniLM-L6-v2" (384 dims, semantic focus)
        """
        self.model_name = model_name
        self.model = SentenceTransformer(model_name)
        self.embedding_dim = self.model.get_sentence_embedding_dimension()
        logger.info(f"Loaded model: {model_name} with {self.embedding_dim} dimensions")

    def embed_text(self, texts: str, batch_size: int = 32) -> np.ndarray:
        """Generate embeddings for a single text input.

        Args:
            texts: A single string to embed.
            batch_size: Batch size for processing (ignored for single text).

        Returns:
            numpy array of shape (embedding_dim,) containing the embedding vector.
            Output dimension matches the model configuration:
                - MiniLM models: 384 dimensions
                - MPNet models: 768 dimensions

        Notes:
            The embedding vector is normalized to unit length.
            Use with cosine similarity for semantic matching.
        """
        if isinstance(texts, str):
            return self._get_single_embedding(texts)
        raise TypeError("Expected str, got single text")

    def embed(self, texts: str, batch_size: int = 32) -> np.ndarray:
        """Public API to generate embeddings for text inputs.

        This is the main method for obtaining embeddings from text.

        Args:
            texts: A single string to convert to an embedding.
            batch_size: Number of texts to process in each batch.

        Returns:
            numpy ndarray of shape (embedding_dim,): normalized embedding vector.

        Example:
            embedder = LocalEmbedder()
            vec = embedder.embed("hello world")
            print(vec.shape)  # (384,)

        Notes:
            Output dimensions are determined by the loaded model:
                - all-MiniLM-L6-v2: (384,)
                - all-mpnet-base-v2: (768,)
        """
        return self.embed_text(texts, batch_size)

    def embed_batch(self, texts: List[str], batch_size: int = 32) -> np.ndarray:

        """
        batch_embeddings = self.model.encode(

        """
        return self.embed_batch(texts)

{
  "summary": "Add graph serialization roundtrip test that creates a graph with mixed attribute types (str, int, float, list), serializes to JSON via to_, deserializes with nx.node_link_graph, and verifies all node and edge attributes are preserved.",
  "file_operations": [
    {
      "path": "tests/test_export.py",
      "action": "write",
      "content": """"Tests for graph export utilities."""

from __future__ import annotations

import 

import networkx as nx
import pytest

from graphify.export import to_


def test__roundtrip_preserves_mixed_attributes():
    """Serializing and deserializing a graph preserves str, int, float, and list attributes.

    Creates a graph where nodes carry string labels, integer counts, float scores,
    and list tags, while edges carry float weights, string labels, and list metadata.
    After a JSON roundtrip through to_ / nx.node_link_graph, every attribute
    on every node and edge must match the original value exactly.
    """
    g = nx.Graph()

    # Add nodes with mixed attribute types
    g.add_node(
        "alpha",
        label="Node Alpha",
        count=42,
        score=3.14,
        tags=["important", "reviewed"],
    )
    g.add_node(
        "beta",
        label="Node Beta",
        count=7,
        score=0.5,
        tags=["new"],
    )
    g.add_node(
        "gamma",
        label="Node Gamma",
        count=0,
        score=-1.5,
        tags=[],
    )

    # Add edges with mixed attribute types
    g.add_edge(
        "alpha",
        "beta",
        weight=1.0,
        label="strong",
        meta=["refactor", "shared-module"],
    )
    g.add_edge(
        "beta",
        "gamma",
        weight=0.3,
        label="weak",
        meta=["indirect"],
    )

    # Serialize to JSON
    _str = to_(g)

    # Verify the output is valid JSON
    data = .loads(_str)

    # Deserialize back into a NetworkX graph
    g_roundtrip = nx.node_link_graph(data)

    # Verify the same set of nodes exists
    assert set(g_roundtrip.nodes()) == {"alpha", "beta", "gamma"}

    # Verify every node attribute is preserved with exact type and value
    for node in g.nodes():
        for key, value in g.nodes[node].items():
            actual = g_roundtrip.nodes[node][key]
            assert actual == value, (
                f"Node '{node}' attribute '{key}': "
                f"expected {value!r} ({type(value).__name__}), "
                f"got {actual!r} ({type(actual).__name__})"
            )

    # Verify the same edges exist
    assert g_roundtrip.has_edge("alpha", "beta")
    assert g_roundtrip.has_edge("beta", "gamma")
    assert g_roundtrip.number_of_edges() == 2

    # Verify every edge attribute is preserved with exact type and value
    for u, v in g.edges():
        for key, value in g[u][v].items():
            actual = g_roundtrip[u][v][key]
            assert actual == value, (
                f"Edge ({u!r}, {v!r}) attribute '{key}': "
                f"expected {value!r} ({type(value).__name__}), "
                f"got {actual!r} ({type(actual).__name__})"
            )
"
    }
  ]
}

text:
[Mined from ablation-harness] Result normalization across multiple LLM output formats: The conditions parse LLM responses from plain text with explicit markers like `ROOT_CAUSE:` and `FILE:`. Although simple, this shows an important normalization tactic: downstream code expects a stable internal shape even when the model output is free-form text. It reduces fragility when swapping prompts or models.

CLAW already routes across models, but this pattern reduces prompt brittleness and parser drift.

## Result normalization across multiple LLM output formats

**Category:** design_patterns
**Source:** ablation-harness
**Relevance:** 0.87

### Description
The conditions parse LLM responses from plain text with explicit markers like `ROOT_CAUSE:` and `FILE:`. Although simple, this shows an important normalization tactic: downstream code expects a stable internal shape even when the model output is free-form text. It reduces fragility when swapping prompts or models.

### Implementation Sketch
CLAW can formalize this into response parsers that map many prompt formats into a stable schema, ideally with pydantic validation. Put it near the LLM adapters so every agent gets normalized outputs regardless of model/provider. This will make multi-model routing and fallback simpler.

### Augmentation Notes
CLAW already routes across models, but this pattern reduces prompt brittleness and parser drift.

{"summary":"Add export format documentation to README.md and fix export.py with proper docstrings for to_json() and new to_html() function","file_operations":[{"path":"src/graphify/export.py","action":"write","content":""""Export utilities for graphify graphs.

Provides functions to serialize NetworkX graphs to various formats
including JSON and HTML for visualization.
"""

import json
from typing import Any

import networkx as nx


class NumpyEncoder(json.JSONEncoder):
    """Custom JSON encoder that converts numpy types to native Python types.

    Handles numpy.integer (int8..int64, uint8..uint64), numpy.floating
    (float16..float128), numpy.bool_, and numpy.ndarray by converting
    them to their native Python equivalents before serialization.
    """

    def default(self, obj: Any) -> Any:
        if hasattr(obj, "dtype"):
            import numpy as np

            if isinstance(obj, np.integer):
                return int(obj)
            if isinstance(obj, np.floating):
                return float(obj)
            if isinstance(obj, np.bool_):
                return bool(obj)
            if isinstance(obj, np.ndarray):
                return obj.tolist()
        return super().default(obj)


def to_json(graph: nx.Graph) -> str:
    """Export a NetworkX graph to a JSON string.

    Converts the graph to node-link data format and serializes it to JSON.
    Graph and node/edge attributes that contain numpy types (e.g. int64,
    float64, ndarray) are automatically converted to native Python types
    via the custom NumpyEncoder.

    Parameters
    ----------
    graph : nx.Graph
        The NetworkX graph to export.

    Returns
    -------
    str
        A JSON string representation of the graph in node-link format.

    Example
    -------
    >>> import networkx as nx
    >>> from graphify.export import to_json
    >>> G = nx.Graph()
    >>> G.add_node("a", label="Node A")
    >>> G.add_node("b", label="Node B")
    >>> G.add_edge("a", "b", weight=1.0)
    >>> print(to_json(G))
    {"directed": false, "multigraph": false, "graph": {}, "nodes": [{"id": "a", "label": "Node A"}, {"id": "b", "label": "Node B"}], "links": [{"source": "a", "target": "b", "weight": 1.0}]}
    """
    data = nx.node_link_data(graph)
    return json.dumps(data, cls=NumpyEncoder)


def to_html(graph: nx.Graph, communities: dict | None = None, title: str = "Graph Visualization") -> str:
    """Export a NetworkX graph to an interactive HTML visualization.

    Generates a standalone HTML file using D3.js to render the graph
    as an interactive force-directed graph. Nodes can be colored by
    community membership when a communities dictionary is provided.

    Parameters
    ----------
    graph : nx.Graph
        The NetworkX graph to export.
    communities : dict | None
        Optional mapping of node identifiers to community labels.
        Nodes are colored by their community when provided.
    title : str
        The title displayed in the HTML page header. Defaults to
        "Graph Visualization".

    Returns
    -------
    str
        A complete HTML document as a string that can be saved to a
        .html file and opened in any web browser.

    Example
    -------
    >>> import networkx as nx
    >>> from graphify.export import to_html
    >>> G = nx.Graph()
    >>> G.add_node("a")
    >>> G.add_node("b")
    >>> G.add_edge("a", "b")
    >>> communities = {"a": 0, "b": 1}
    >>> html = to_html(G, communities=communities, title="My Graph")
    >>> with open("graph.html", "w") as f:
    ...     f.write(html)
    """
    nodes_data = []
    for node in graph.nodes():
        node_info = {
            "id": node,
            "group": communities.get(node, 0) if communities else 0,
        }
        # Include node attributes
        for key, value in graph.nodes[node].items():
            if key != "group":
                node_info[key] = value
        nodes_data.append(node_info)

    links_data = []
    for source, target in graph.edges():
        edge_info = {"source": source, "target": target}
        # Include edge attributes
        for key, value in graph.edges[source, target].items():
            edge_info[key] = value
        links_data.append(edge_info)

    graph_json = json.dumps({"nodes": nodes_data, "links": links_data})

    # Generate unique community colors if communities provided
    community_colors = ""
    if communities:
        unique_communities = sorted(set(communities.values()))
        color_scale = [
            "#1f77b4", "#ff7f0e", "#2ca02c", "#d62728", "#9467bd",
            "#8c564b", "#e377c2", "#7f7f7f", "#bcbd22", "#17becf",
        ]
        community_colors = ", ".join(
            f"{c}: '{color_scale[i % len(color_scale)]}'"
            for i, c in enumerate(unique_communities)
        )

    html_content = f'''<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
    <title>{title}</title>
    <script

from collections import defaultdict
from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime
from typing import Dict, Optional
import time


@dataclass
class RateStats:
    """Rate limit statistics for a given key."""
    allowed: int
    denied: int
    tokens: float
    last_seen: datetime


class RateLimiter:
    """Token-bucket rate limiter.

    Supports configurable sustained rate (tokens per second) and burst size.
    Uses continuous token replenishment based on elapsed time.
    """

    def __init__(self, sustained: float = 10.0, burst: int = 20):
        """Initialize rate limiter.

        Args:
            sustained: Sustained rate in tokens per second (default 10.0).
            burst: Maximum burst size in tokens (default 20).
        """
        if sustained <= 0 or burst <= 0:
            raise ValueError("sustained and burst must be positive")

        self.sustained = sustained
        self.burst = float(burst)
        self._state: Dict[str, Dict] = defaultdict(lambda: {
            'tokens': self.burst,
            'last_time': time.time(),
            'allowed': 0,
            'denied': 0
        })

    def allow(self, key: str) -> bool:
        """Check if the given key is allowed to proceed.

        Replenishes tokens based on elapsed time then consumes one if available.

        Args:
            key: Identifier for the rate limit bucket (e.g. IP, user ID).

        Returns:
            True if the request is allowed, False otherwise.
        """
        now = time.time()
        state = self._state[key]

        # Replenish tokens based on time passed
        elapsed = now - state['last_time']
        state['tokens'] = min(
            self.burst,
            state['tokens'] + elapsed * self.sustained
        )
        state['last_time'] = now

        if state['tokens'] < 1.0:
            self._update_stats(key, False, now, state['tokens'])
            return False

        # Consume token
        state['tokens'] -= 1.0
        self._update_stats(key, True, now, state['tokens'])
        return True

    def get_stats(self, key: str) -> RateStats:
        """Return usage statistics for a key.

        Creates default stats if key has never been seen.

        Args:
            key: The key to retrieve stats for.

        Returns:
            RateStats object with current usage information.
        """
        if key not in self._state:
            return RateStats(
                allowed=0,
                denied=0,
                tokens=0.0,
                last_seen=datetime.fromtimestamp(0)
            )
        state = self._state[key]
        # Return current tokens (updated on allow; approximate here)
        tokens = state['tokens']
        return RateStats(
            allowed=state['allowed'],
            denied=state['denied'],
            tokens=tokens,
            last_seen=datetime.fromtimestamp(state['last_time'])
        )

    def _update_stats(self, key: str, allowed: bool, now: float, tokens: float) -> None:
        """Update internal statistics for a key."""
        state = self._state[key]
        if allowed:
            state['allowed'] += 1
        else:
            state['denied'] += 1
        state['tokens'] = tokens
        state['last_time'] = now

    def reset(self, key: Optional[str] = None) -> None:
        """Reset state for a key or all keys (for testing)."""
        if key is None:
            self._state.clear()
        elif key in self._state:
            del self._state[key]

A small standalone Python app that generates a risky decision scenario and lets the user choose a response.

## Features

- Generates randomized risky scenarios
- Scores user choices by risk level
- Works from the command line
- Includes automated tests for the main workflow

## Requirements

- Python 3.11+

## Run the app

```bash
python risky_app.py
```

## Run tests

```bash
python -m pytest
```

## How it works

The app prints a scenario with several possible actions. Each action has a hidden risk score. The selected action is evaluated and the app reports the outcome.
"},{"path":"risky_app.py","action":"write","content":""""Risky App CLI.

A small standalone app that generates a risky scenario and evaluates a user's
choice.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

from dataclasses import dataclass
from random import Random
from typing import Iterable


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Choice:
    label: str
    risk: int
    outcome: str


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Scenario:
    title: str
    prompt: str
    choices: tuple[Choice, ...]


SCENARIOS: tuple[Scenario, ...] = (
    Scenario(
        title="Night Ferry",
        prompt="A storm is building and the ferry may be delayed. What do you do?",
        choices=(
            Choice("Board immediately", 8, "You arrive sooner, but the crossing is rough."),
            Choice("Wait for the next departure", 3, "You lose time, but avoid the roughest weather."),
            Choice("Take a small boat shortcut", 10, "Fast, but highly unsafe in storm conditions."),
        ),
    ),
    Scenario(
        title="Startup Launch",
        prompt="Your team can ship now with known bugs or delay for polishing. What's your move?",
        choices=(
            Choice("Ship now", 7, "Users see the product early, but support load spikes."),
            Choice("Delay one week", 4, "You fix more issues and reduce launch-day surprises."),
            Choice("Rewrite everything", 9, "Potentially cleaner, but the project slips badly."),
        ),
    ),
    Scenario(
        title="Mountain Pass",
        prompt="A shortcut over the pass may save hours, but the trail is unstable.",
        choices=(
            Choice("Take the pass", 9, "You save time if conditions hold, but a slide is possible."),
            Choice("Use the main road", 2, "Slower, but much safer."),
            Choice("Turn back", 1, "Safest option, but you lose the journey's progress."),
        ),
    ),
)


def pick_scenario(rng: Random | None = None) -> Scenario:
    rng = rng or Random()
    return rng.choice(SCENARIOS)


def evaluate_choice(choice: Choice) -> str:
    if choice.risk >= 8:
        return f"High risk: {choice.outcome}"
    if choice.risk >= 5:
        return f"Moderate risk: {choice.outcome}"
    return f"Low risk: {choice.outcome}"


def render_scenario(scenario: Scenario) -> str:
    lines = [f"=== {scenario.title} ===", scenario.prompt, ""]
    for idx, choice in enumerate(scenario.choices, start=1):
        lines.append(f"{idx}. {choice.label}")
    return "\n".join(lines)


def run_interactive(input_func=input, print_func=print, rng: Random | None = None) -> int:
    scenario = pick_scenario(rng)
    print_func(render_scenario(scenario))
    print_func("")
    raw = input_func("Choose 1-3: ").strip()
    try:
        index = int(raw) - 1
        choice = scenario.choices[index]
    except (ValueError, IndexError):
        print_func("Invalid choice. The risky situation passes without action.")
        return 1

    print_func(evaluate_choice(choice))
    return 0


def main() -> int:
    return run_interactive()


if __name__ == "__main__":
    raise SystemExit(main())

      "path": "app/cli.py",
      "action": "write",
      "content": """"Command-line interface for generating markdown checklists."""

import argparse
import sys
from typing import List, Optional

from app import __version__


def create_checklist(title: str, items: List[str]) -> str:
    """Generate a markdown checklist from title and items.

    Args:
        title: The title for the checklist
        items: List of bullet items (should be exactly 3)

    Returns:
        Formatted markdown string
    """
    if len(items) != 3:
        raise ValueError("Exactly 3 items required")

    lines = [f"# {title}"]
    for item in items:
        lines.append(f"- [ ] {item}")
    return "\n".join(lines)


def parse_args(argv: Optional[List[str]] = None) -> argparse.Namespace:
    """Parse command line arguments.

    Args:
        argv: Command line arguments (defaults to sys.argv[1:])

    Returns:
        Parsed arguments namespace
    """
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(
        description="Generate a markdown checklist from a title and 3 bullet items.",
        formatter_class=argparse.RawDescriptionHelpFormatter,
        epilog="""Examples:
  python -m app.cli --title "Weekly Tasks" --item "Buy groceries" --item "Clean house" --item "Read book"
  python -m app.cli -t "Meeting Agenda" -i "Discuss Q1 results" -i "Plan Q2 goals" -i "Team feedback"""
    )

    parser.add_argument(
        "-t", "--title",
        required=True,
        help="Title for the checklist"
    )

    parser.add_argument(
        "-i", "--item",
        action="append",
        dest="items",
        required=True,
        help="A bullet item (exactly 3 required)"
    )

    parser.add_argument(
        "-v", "--version",
        action="version",
        version=f"app.cli {__version__}"
    )

    return parser.parse_args(argv)


def main(argv: Optional[List[str]] = None) -> int:
    """Main entry point for the CLI.

    Args:
        argv: Command line arguments (defaults to sys.argv[1:])

    Returns:
        Exit code (0 for success, non-zero for errors)
    """
    try:
        args = parse_args(argv)

        if len(args.items) != 3:

  "file_operations": [
    {
      "path": "src/graphify/cluster.py",
      "action": "write",
      "content": """"Graph clustering utilities for the graphify package.

Provides functions to detect communities and clusters within
NetworkX graph structures using various algorithms.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import networkx as nx
from typing import Any, Dict, List, Optional, Set, Tuple


def detect_communities(
    graph: nx.Graph,
    algorithm: str = "louvain",
    resolution: float = 1.0,
    random_state: Optional[int] = None,
) -> Dict[Any, int]:
    """Detect communities (clusters) in a graph.

    Parameters
    ----------
    graph : nx.Graph
        The input graph to cluster.
    algorithm : str
        Community detection algorithm to use. Supported values:
        ``"louvain"``, ``"label_propagation"``, ``"greedy_modularity"``.
        Defaults to ``"louvain"``.
    resolution : float
        Resolution parameter for algorithms that support it (e.g. Louvain).
        Higher values yield smaller communities. Defaults to ``1.0``.
    random_state : Optional[int]
        Seed for deterministic results in stochastic algorithms.

    Returns
    -------
    Dict[Any, int]
        Mapping from node to community label (integer).
    """
    if not graph.nodes():
        return {}

    if algorithm == "louvain":
        try:
            from networkx.algorithms.community import louvain_communities

            kwargs: Dict[str, Any] = {"resolution": resolution}
            if random_state is not None:
                kwargs["seed"] = random_state
            communities = louvain_communities(graph, **kwargs)
            node_to_community: Dict[Any, int] = {}
            for idx, community in enumerate(communities):
                for node in community:
                    node_to_community[node] = idx
            return node_to_community
        except ImportError:
            raise ValueError(
                "Louvain algorithm requires networkx>=2.7. "
                "Install or upgrade networkx to use this algorithm."
            )
    elif algorithm == "label_propagation":
        from networkx.algorithms.community import label_propagation_communities

        communities = label_propagation_communities(graph)
        node_to_community = {}
        for idx, community in enumerate(communities):
            for node in community:
                node_to_community[node] = idx
        return node_to_community
    elif algorithm == "greedy_modularity":
        from networkx.algorithms.community import greedy_modularity_communities

        communities = greedy_modularity_communities(graph, resolution=resolution)
        node_to_community = {}
        for idx, community in enumerate(communities):
            for node in community:
                node_to_community[node] = idx
        return node_to_community
    else:
        raise ValueError(
            f"Unknown algorithm '{algorithm}'. "
            f"Supported: 'louvain', 'label_propagation', 'greedy_modularity'"
        )


def cluster_summary(
    node_to_community: Dict[Any, int],
) -> List[Dict[str, Any]]:
    """Produce a human-readable summary of detected clusters.

    Parameters
    ----------
    node_to_community : Dict[Any, int]
        Mapping from node to community label, as returned by
        :func:`detect_communities`.

    Returns
    -------
    List[Dict[str, Any]]
        A list of dicts, one per community, each containing:

        - ``"id"`` – the community label (int)
        - ``"size"`` – number of nodes in the community (int)
        - ``"nodes"`` – list of nodes belonging to the community (list)
    """
    communities: Dict[int, List[Any]] = {}
    for node, label in node_to_community.items():
        communities.setdefault(label, []).append(node)

    summary: List[Dict[str, Any]] = []
    for label in sorted(communities):
        nodes = communities[label]
        summary.append({
            "id": label,
            "size": len(nodes),
            "nodes": nodes,
        })
    return summary


def intra_cluster_density(
    graph: nx.Graph,
    node_to_community: Dict[Any, int],
) -> Dict[int, float]:
    """Compute the density of each cluster (sub-graph induced by community nodes).

    Parameters
    ----------
    graph : nx.Graph
        The full graph.
    node_to_community : Dict[Any, int]
        Mapping from node to community label.

    Returns
    -------
    Dict[int, float]
        Mapping from community label to its sub-graph density.
        Communities with fewer than 2 nodes have density ``0.0``.
    """
    communities: Dict[int, Set[Any]] = {}
    for node, label in node_to_community.items():
        communities.setdefault(label, set()).add(node)

    densities: Dict[int, float] = {}
    for label, nodes in communities.items():
        if len(nodes) < 2:
            densities[label] = 0.0
            continue
        subgraph = graph.subgraph(nodes)
        n = len(nodes)
        possible = n * (n - 1) / 2
        actual = subgraph.number_of_edges()
